citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "redis_token_store_degradation" title: "Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "redis-token-store, auth-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "redis, session, token, auth, cache" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook Use this runbook when login succeeds intermittently, sessions disappear, token validation times out, or Redis latency/evictions rise. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check Redis `PING`, primary role, replica health, memory usage, and eviction count. 2. Compare Redis latency with auth-service token validation errors. 3. Check whether a recent auth-service deployment changed token write volume or TTL. 4. Inspect network errors between auth-service pods and Redis. 5. Confirm whether failover is active, partial, or stuck. ## Triage Decision Tree - If Redis memory is above 90% and evictions are increasing, reduce token write pressure and scale memory. - If primary is unavailable, follow the managed Redis failover process. - If only auth-service sees errors, inspect client pool size and connection reuse. ## Remediation 1. Disable noisy token refresh paths or reduce refresh frequency if introduced by deployment. 2. Scale Redis memory or shard capacity if safe and pre-approved. 3. Restart auth-service clients only after confirming Redis itself is healthy. 4. Avoid `FLUSHDB` in production unless the incident commander and Identity owner approve. ## Verification - Redis latency returns to normal. - Evictions stop increasing. - Login success and token validation rates recover. ## Escalation Escalate to Identity and Access for auth impact and Data Platform if Redis failover or capacity changes are required.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "similar_incidents_examples" title: "Similar Incident Examples" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "similar incidents, examples, triage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Similar Incident Examples ## Login Failure Similarity Signals - Same service: auth-service. - Same timing: failure starts after production deployment. - Same symptoms: login error rate, token validation failure, session loss, 401 for valid users. - Same dependencies: Redis token store, customer-db, API Gateway. ## Gateway 5xx Similarity Signals - Same public route or stage. - Gateway 5xx mirrors backend 5xx. - Recent route, WAF, certificate, or integration timeout change. ## Database Similarity Signals - CPU high, lock waits, connection pool saturation, migration locks, replica lag. - New query pattern after deployment.

triage_card.answer:
**Priority:** Likely **P2** (possibly P1 if wallet transactions are failing) pending business impact confirmation. Replication lag after deployment in a UKGC-regulated environment affecting wallet-service requires urgent attention due to potential financial transaction impact.

**Investigation findings:**
- **Service owner:** Wallet Platform team, escalation path: Primary Wallet Platform On-Call → Secondary Wallet Platform On-Call (PD-WALL-001)
- **No similar past incidents** found for wallet-service with this symptom
- **Symptom pattern:** Replication lag post-deployment suggests increased write load, new query pattern, or database migration introduced by the wallet-service release

**Triage plan** (reversible steps first):

1. **Check replica lag metrics and database health** - confirm lag magnitude, identify which replica(s), check primary CPU/connections/lock waits (Database Connectivity Runbook)
2. **Correlate deployment timing** - identify wallet-service deployment version and exact time (requires alert timestamp)
3. **Inspect query patterns** - check for new queries, missing indexes, or long transactions introduced by deployment
4. **Decision point:** If replica lag affects reads only, remove lagging replica from rotation; if write load increased, assess rollback vs. query optimization
5. **If deployment is within 60 minutes and metrics degraded post-release:** Prepare rollback while checking database migration compatibility (Deployment Rollback Runbook)
6. **Do NOT blindly roll back database migrations** - involve DBA and Wallet Platform owner if schema changes are present

**Escalation recommendation:**
- **Immediate:** Notify Wallet Platform On-Call (primary owner)
- **If P1/P2 confirmed:** Escalate to Platform DBA for database investigation
- **Required for rollback approval:** Wallet Platform owner, Platform DBA (if migrations present), and Release owner

**Resolution plan:**
1. Stop deployment if rollback is chosen
2. Execute approved rollback or remove lagging replica from rotation
3. Verify: replica lag returns to normal, wallet transaction success rate recovers, no new alerts
4. Follow-up: Post-incident review with query analysis and migration safety validation

**Business impact:** Not yet quantified - requires severity classification. Wallet-service in GIB-UKGC handles financial transactions; prolonged replication lag may cause stale balance reads or transaction processing delays.

**Confidence:** Medium - runbook guidance is clear, but I need:
- Alert timestamp to correlate deployment timing
- Actual lag magnitude and customer impact to classify severity
- Database metrics (CPU, connections, query patterns) to determine root cause

**Next step:** Please provide the alert timestamp so I can correlate recent wallet-service deployments.

**Symptom pattern:** Replication lag post-deployment suggests increased write load, new query pattern, or database migration introduced by the wallet-service release
**Check replica lag metrics and database health** - confirm lag magnitude, identify which replica(s), check primary CPU/connections/lock waits (Database Connectivity Runbook)
**Correlate deployment timing** - identify wallet-service deployment version and exact time (requires alert timestamp)
**Inspect query patterns** - check for new queries, missing indexes, or long transactions introduced by deployment
**Decision point:** If replica lag affects reads only, remove lagging replica from rotation; if write load increased, assess rollback vs. query optimization
**If deployment is within 60 minutes and metrics degraded post-release:** Prepare rollback while checking database migration compatibility (Deployment Rollback Runbook)

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "redis_token_store_degradation" title: "Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "redis-token-store, auth-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "redis, session, token, auth, cache" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook Use this runbook when login succeeds intermittently, sessions disappear, token validation times out, or Redis latency/evictions rise. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check Redis `PING`, primary role, replica health, memory usage, and eviction count. 2. Compare Redis latency with auth-service token validation errors. 3. Check whether a recent auth-service deployment changed token write volume or TTL. 4. Inspect network errors between auth-service pods and Redis. 5. Confirm whether failover is active, partial, or stuck. ## Triage Decision Tree - If Redis memory is above 90% and evictions are increasing, reduce token write pressure and scale memory. - If primary is unavailable, follow the managed Redis failover process. - If only auth-service sees errors, inspect client pool size and connection reuse. ## Remediation 1. Disable noisy token refresh paths or reduce refresh frequency if introduced by deployment. 2. Scale Redis memory or shard capacity if safe and pre-approved. 3. Restart auth-service clients only after confirming Redis itself is healthy. 4. Avoid `FLUSHDB` in production unless the incident commander and Identity owner approve. ## Verification - Redis latency returns to normal. - Evictions stop increasing. - Login success and token validation rates recover. ## Escalation Escalate to Identity and Access for auth impact and Data Platform if Redis failover or capacity changes are required.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "similar_incidents_examples" title: "Similar Incident Examples" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "similar incidents, examples, triage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Similar Incident Examples ## Login Failure Similarity Signals - Same service: auth-service. - Same timing: failure starts after production deployment. - Same symptoms: login error rate, token validation failure, session loss, 401 for valid users. - Same dependencies: Redis token store, customer-db, API Gateway. ## Gateway 5xx Similarity Signals - Same public route or stage. - Gateway 5xx mirrors backend 5xx. - Recent route, WAF, certificate, or integration timeout change. ## Database Similarity Signals - CPU high, lock waits, connection pool saturation, migration locks, replica lag. - New query pattern after deployment.

triage_card.answer:
**Priority: P2** (Potential P1 depending on lag duration and customer impact)

Replication lag on wallet-service in GIB-UKGC is a high-severity issue due to:
- ~12,000 active users affected
- $1,764/min ($105,840/hr) potential revenue impact
- High SLA and regulatory risk (UKGC, DGE compliance)
- Financial transaction consistency concerns

**Investigation findings:**

Owner: Wallet Platform team
Escalation: Primary Wallet Platform On-Call → Secondary Wallet Platform On-Call (PD-WALL-001)

Database replication lag on a wallet service can cause:
- Stale balance reads
- Delayed transaction visibility
- Potential financial reconciliation issues
- Read/write inconsistency

According to the Database Connectivity Runbook, when replica lag affects reads, the immediate triage is to remove the lagging replica from rotation. The runbook emphasizes checking database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, slow queries, and comparing with recent deployment timeline.

**Triage plan (Database Connectivity Runbook):**

1. **Immediate validation** - Check replica lag metrics:
   - How far behind is the replica (seconds/minutes)?
   - Is lag increasing or stable?
   - Check primary database CPU, memory, active connections

2. **Remove lagging replica from rotation** - If replica lag is confirmed and affecting reads, immediately remove the lagging replica from the read pool to prevent stale data reads

3. **Check for deployment correlation** - Compare recent wallet-service or database deployments with lag onset:
   - New queries or ORM changes that increased replication load
   - Schema migrations creating locks
   - Increased write volume from feature releases

4. **Inspect replication health**:
   - Check replication slot status
   - Verify network connectivity between primary and replica
   - Check for blocking queries or long-running transactions on primary
   - Review slow query logs for expensive reads

5. **Query pattern analysis** - Identify any new query patterns or missing indexes causing write amplification

**Escalation recommendation:**

Escalate to **Platform DBA immediately** for P2 database incidents. Include:
- Service: wallet-service
- Environment: GIB-UKGC
- Symptom: Replication lag exceeded threshold
- Business impact: $105,840/hr, UKGC regulatory exposure
- Deployment correlation status (pending alert time)

Notify Wallet Platform On-Call team given high regulatory and financial risk.

**Resolution verification:**

Per Database Connectivity Runbook:
- Replica lag returns to normal (< threshold)
- No new lock waits accumulate
- Wallet-service read/write success metrics recover
- Connection pool saturation clears
- Monitor for at least 10 minutes after remediation
- Verify transaction consistency and balance accuracy

**Next steps needed:**
- Alert timestamp for deployment correlation
- Current replica lag value and trend
- Primary database resource utilization
- Recent wallet-service deployment history

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5. Validate successful login rate and token issuance before declaring mitigation. ## Verification - `auth_login_success_rate` returns to baseline. - `auth_login_error_rate` stays below 1% for 10 minutes. - Redis token writes and reads are stable. - Synthetic login tests pass in production. ## Escalation Escalate P1/P2 login incidents to Identity and Access primary on-call, then secondary on-call if no acknowledgement within the policy window. Produce a safe escalation preview only; do not send real escalation messages automatically.

--- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

triage_card.answer:
**Priority: P1**  
Many users unable to log in represents critical impact: ~50,000 active users affected, $14,000/min revenue loss ($840,000/hr), regulatory exposure (UKGC, DGE), and critical SLA breach risk.

**Investigation findings:**

- **Service owner:** Identity & Access team (PD-AUTH-001)
- **Deployment correlation:** No recent deployments found in the last 6 hours for auth-service or dependencies (redis-token-store, customer-db). However, you stated "latest production deployment" – **I need the exact deployment time or alert start time to confirm correlation.**
- **Similar incidents:** 
  - INC-2025-0019 (P2): Bad deployment configuration caused dependency timeouts, resolved by updating connection limits (68 min MTTR)
  - INC-2025-0022 (P3): Provider throttling caused dependency timeout, resolved by scaling workers (93 min MTTR)
  - Historical pattern: Auth login failures after deployments often trace to Redis token store exhaustion, connection pool saturation, or invalid API Gateway routes

**Triage plan (ordered, reversible steps):**

1. **Freeze deployments** – Halt all auth-service releases until incident commander approves.
2. **Confirm deployment correlation** – Verify if login failures started within 60 minutes of the latest deployment.
3. **Check auth-service pod health** – Validate readiness probes and `/health/ready` responses.
4. **Check Redis token store** – Confirm latency, evictions, memory pressure, primary/replica status.
5. **Check customer-db connectivity** – Look for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, migration locks.
6. **Verify API Gateway routes** – Ensure OIDC discovery and public routes return valid responses.
7. **Rollback decision** – If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback. Prefer feature flag rollback if only a new code path is affected. **Do not roll back database migrations without DBA approval.**
8. **Disable new feature flags** – If present, disable flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior.
9. **Restart unhealthy pods** – Capture logs first, then restart only unhealthy auth-service pods.

**Escalation recommendation:**  
**Immediate escalation required** – P1 incident with $14,000/min revenue impact and regulatory exposure.  
- **Primary:** Identity & Access On-Call (PagerDuty PD-AUTH-001)  
- **Secondary:** Identity & Access Secondary On-Call  
- **Also notify:** Incident Commander, Platform Release Owner

Would you like me to preview the escalation notification? (I will NOT send live notifications without explicit confirmation.)

**Resolution & validation:**

After mitigation (rollback or remediation):
- Monitor `auth_login_success_rate` returns to baseline
- Confirm `auth_login_error_rate` stays below 1% for 10 minutes
- Verify Redis token writes/reads are stable
- Run synthetic login tests in production
- Open follow-up ticket for failed release before unfreezing deployments

**Business impact:**  
$840,000/hour revenue loss, 50,000 users affected, critical SLA breach, high regulatory risk (UKGC, DGE), high reputation risk.

**Confidence: MEDIUM** – Strong runbook and historical incident evidence, but **I need the exact deployment time to confirm deployment correlation.** The deployment lookup returned no deploys in the last 6 hours, which conflicts with your statement "after the latest production deployment."

**Action needed:** Please provide the exact deployment timestamp or alert start time so I can confirm deployment correlation and refine the rollback decision.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "business_impact_guide" title: "Business Impact Interpretation" doc_type: "guide" services: "all" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE, MGM, MIRAGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3,P4" tags: "business_impact, tier, revenue, compliance" last_updated: "2026-06-09" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- Use the piter-service-context Action Group and data/source/business_impact.json for numeric impact scores, tier labels, and environment-specific revenue exposure. ## How to explain impact - Tier-0 services (auth-service, wallet-service, bet-service, payments-service, replication) block revenue or regulated play when degraded in production. - Auth-service login failures in GIB-UKGC affect regulated UKGC traffic; cite tier-0 and login dependency on Redis token store. - Always pair narrative impact with severity (P1-P4) and environment code from the alert. Do not invent dollar amounts; quote Action Group fields or say data is unavailable.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "payment_service" title: "Payment Service Context" doc_type: "service" services: "payment-service, payments-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "service ownership, payments, provider" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Payment Service Context Payment-service owns authorization, provider routing, deposit success, withdrawal processing, and payment-provider failover. ## Owner - Team: Payments - Primary escalation: Primary Payments On-Call - Secondary escalation: Secondary Payments On-Call - Channel: `#payments` ## Dependencies - External payment gateway. - Wallet service. - API Gateway. ## Business Impact Payment incidents can cause direct revenue loss, customer trust damage, and PCI-DSS/compliance follow-up.

triage_card.answer:
**Priority: P1 CRITICAL**
100% error rate on bet-service in GIB-UKGC with unresponsive nodes. This is a revenue-blocking, tier-0, regulatory-exposed incident.

**Business Impact:**
- ~32,000 active users affected
- $9,800/minute ($588,000/hour revenue loss)
- Critical SLA breach (99.95% target)
- High regulatory exposure: UKGC, DGE, MGM
- Reputation risk: high

**Investigation Findings:**

No recent deployments found in last 6 hours to bet-service or dependencies (wallet-service, auth-service, game-service, replication). This rules out deployment-related root cause.

**Similar incident pattern identified (INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE):**
- Same service, environment, and symptom: 100% error rate
- Root cause: Connection pool exhaustion from retry storm against wallet dependency
- MTTR: 27 minutes
- Resolution: Disabled retry fan-out, restarted bet-service pool, validated wallet latency

Additional similar patterns from past incidents suggest dependency latency and connection pool saturation as primary causes when deployment correlation is absent.

**Triage Plan (immediate actions):**

1. **Check wallet-service health and latency** - bet-service depends on wallet-service; validate wallet-service error rate, p99 latency, and health endpoints
   
2. **Inspect bet-service connection pool metrics** - check for pool exhaustion, active connections, retry queue depth, and circuit breaker state

3. **Capture diagnostic logs before restart** - collect logs from unresponsive nodes showing connection failures, timeouts, or retry patterns

4. **Execute rolling restart of bet-service pods** - restart saturated/unresponsive nodes after capturing diagnostics to restore service

5. **Validate circuit breaker configuration** - ensure retry logic has appropriate circuit breaker gates to prevent future retry storms

6. **Monitor bet placement success rate** - validate recovery with end-to-end bet placement checks for at least 10 minutes

**Escalation Recommendation:**

**IMMEDIATE ESCALATION REQUIRED (P1)**
- Owner Team: Betting Core
- Escalation Path: Primary Betting Core On-Call → Secondary Betting Core On-Call
- PagerDuty Service: PD-BET--001
- Notify: Primary on-call, Secondary on-call, Team Lead, Incident Commander
- Channels: SNS, SES

Given regulatory exposure and $9,800/minute revenue loss, escalate immediately to Betting Core on-call and stand up incident commander.

**Resolution Plan:**

After restart and stabilization:
1. Confirm error rate drops below 1%
2. Validate p99 latency returns to baseline
3. Check wallet-service dependency health remains stable
4. Verify circuit breaker thresholds are appropriate
5. Monitor for 15+ minutes before declaring resolved

**Post-incident:**
- Root cause analysis focused on connection pool configuration and retry behavior
- Review circuit breaker gates and retry limits
- Update monitoring to alert on pool saturation before node exhaustion

**Confidence and Uncertainty:**

**Confidence: High** on triage approach based on:
- Strong match to historical incident INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE (same service, environment, symptoms)
- No deployment correlation rules out release-related causes
- Connection pool exhaustion pattern documented in similar incidents

**Uncertainty: Medium** on specific root cause until diagnostics confirm:
- Whether wallet-service is degraded or bet-service retry logic is misconfigured
- Whether external factors (network, infrastructure) are contributing

**Next diagnostic priority:** Wallet-service health check and bet-service connection pool metrics will resolve uncertainty.

Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "business_impact_guide" title: "Business Impact Interpretation" doc_type: "guide" services: "all" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE, MGM, MIRAGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3,P4" tags: "business_impact, tier, revenue, compliance" last_updated: "2026-06-09" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- Use the piter-service-context Action Group and data/source/business_impact.json for numeric impact scores, tier labels, and environment-specific revenue exposure. ## How to explain impact - Tier-0 services (auth-service, wallet-service, bet-service, payments-service, replication) block revenue or regulated play when degraded in production. - Auth-service login failures in GIB-UKGC affect regulated UKGC traffic; cite tier-0 and login dependency on Redis token store. - Always pair narrative impact with severity (P1-P4) and environment code from the alert. Do not invent dollar amounts; quote Action Group fields or say data is unavailable.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "piter_workflow" title: "PITER Incident Response Workflow" doc_type: "policy" services: "all" environments: "production" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3,P4" tags: "PITER, workflow, priority, investigation, triage, escalation, resolution" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # PITER Incident Response Workflow PITER stands for Priority, Investigation, Triage, Escalation, Resolution. ## Priority Classify severity using customer impact, error rate, business criticality, revenue exposure, and regulated-market risk. ## Investigation Collect alert details, service context, recent deployments, dependency health, similar incidents, and Knowledge Base citations. ## Triage Choose the safest next checks and mitigations. Prefer reversible changes such as rollback, feature-flag disablement, or traffic shift when evidence supports them. ## Escalation Recommend who should be notified and produce a safe preview. Require human confirmation before any live notification. ## Resolution Verify metrics, document timeline, preserve evidence, update runbooks, and create follow-up actions.

--- doc_id: "escalation_policies_guide" title: "PITER Escalation Policy" doc_type: "policy" services: "all" environments: "production" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3,P4" tags: "escalation, safe preview, notification, guardrails, escalation_policies" last_updated: "2026-06-09" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- PITER may recommend escalation and draft safe escalation previews. It must not send real escalation messages automatically. Structured role mappings and PagerDuty schedule IDs live in data/source/escalation_policies.json (piter-escalation Action Group). Use that tool for who to page; use this guide for severity rules and safe preview wording. ## Severity Policy - P1: notify primary on-call, secondary on-call, incident commander, and service lead immediately. - P2: notify primary on-call and service lead within 15 minutes. - P3: notify primary on-call during business hours unless customer impact increases. - P4: ticket only unless trend worsens. ## Safe Escalation Preview Guidelines 1. Include service, environment, priority, impact, suspected cause, and next action. 2. Mask direct recipient details. 3. Do not include real phone numbers or personal email addresses. 4. Do not expose secrets, tokens, AWS keys, or private credentials. 5. Say when information is missing instead of inventing facts. ## Confirmation Human confirmation is required before SNS, SES, Slack, PagerDuty, or other live notifications.

# PITER AiOps Knowledge Base This knowledge base contains the qualitative operational documents used by PITER AiOps for Retrieval Augmented Generation. PITER stands for: - Priority - Investigation - Triage - Escalation - Resolution ## Scope The knowledge base should contain procedures, runbooks, incident lessons, service descriptions, escalation guidance, and business impact guidance. It should not be the single source of truth for exact structured values such as on-call rotations, deployment rows, incident IDs, dollar impact numbers, or escalation targets. Those values should come from Action Groups that read structured data from CSV and JSON files. ## Folder layout ```text knowledge_base/ runbooks/ incidents/ services/ piter/ escalation/ business_impact/ ``` ## Relationship with Action Groups Use the Knowledge Base for: - Troubleshooting procedures - Triage reasoning - Safe remediation guidance - Service behavior descriptions - Incident learning patterns - Escalation policy explanation - Business impact explanation Use Action Groups for: - Recent deployments from deploys.csv - Service owners and Slack channels from service_owners.csv - Business impact numbers from business_impact.json - Similar incident rows from past_incidents.csv - Escalation targets from escalation_policies.json - Priority classification rules from priority_matrix.json ## Grounding rules - If a fact is not in the Knowledge Base or Action Group result, say "Not in knowledge base". - Do not invent owners, contacts, incidents, dollar impact, deployment versions, or escalation targets. - Prefer safe and reversible actions first. - Dangerous actions require human approval.

triage_card.answer:
**Priority: P1 — Critical**

This is a P1 incident on auth-service in production. Many users cannot log in, which blocks revenue and regulated play. Business impact: ~50,000 active users affected, $840,000/hour revenue loss, critical SLA risk, and high regulatory exposure (UKGC, DGE).

**Investigation findings:**

• **Service owner:** Identity & Access team  
• **Escalation path:** Primary Identity & Access On-Call → Secondary Identity & Access On-Call  
• **Recent deployments:** No auth-service or dependent deployments (redis-token-store, customer-db) found in the past 6 hours. The user reports login failures after "the latest deployment," but deployment records do not show a recent release. **Action needed:** Confirm the actual deployment time and version.  
• **Similar incidents:** INC-2025-0019 (P2, MIRAGE) was caused by bad deployment configuration, resolved with connection limit updates and service restart (MTTR 68 min). INC-2025-0022 (P3, GIB-UKGC) was caused by provider throttling, resolved by scaling workers and clearing queue (MTTR 93 min).  
• **Key dependencies:** Redis token store and customer-db health must be checked.

**Triage plan (ordered, reversible steps):**

1. **Freeze deployments** for auth-service until incident commander approves further releases.  
2. **Verify deployment timing:** Confirm the exact auth-service deployment version and time. If deployment occurred within the last 60 minutes and failures started immediately after, prepare rollback.  
3. **Check auth-service pod health:** Validate readiness probes and `/health/ready` responses. Capture logs from any unhealthy pods before restarting them.  
4. **Check Redis token store:** Verify latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. If Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook.  
5. **Check customer-db connectivity:** Look for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, or migration locks. If present, follow the database connectivity runbook.  
6. **Validate API Gateway routes:** Confirm OIDC discovery and public gateway routes are returning valid responses. If gateway returns 5xx while auth-service pods are healthy, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook.  
7. **Roll back deployment** if strong deployment correlation exists and rollback is available. Prefer feature-flag rollback if only a new code path is affected. Do not roll back database migrations without DBA and owning team involvement.  
8. **Validate recovery:** Watch `auth_login_success_rate` return to baseline and `auth_login_error_rate` stay below 1% for 10 minutes. Confirm synthetic login tests pass.

**Escalation recommendation:**

For P1 on auth-service, immediately notify:  
• Primary Identity & Access On-Call (PagerDuty service PD-AUTH-001)  
• Secondary Identity & Access On-Call (if no acknowledgement within policy window)  
• Incident commander  
• Platform release owner (for rollback approval)

Preview mode is active by default. Real escalation messages require explicit confirmation.

**Resolution plan:**

1. After mitigation, verify `auth_login_success_rate`, `auth_login_error_rate`, Redis token writes/reads, and synthetic login tests remain stable for at least 10 minutes.  
2. Confirm no new alerts fire for auth-service.  
3. Document timeline, preserve logs and metrics, and open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments.  
4. Update runbook if new patterns emerge.

**Business impact:**

• Revenue impact: $840,000/hour ($14,000/minute)  
• ~50,000 active users affected  
• SLA risk: critical (99.95% uptime target)  
• Regulatory exposure: UKGC, DGE (high risk)  
• Reputation risk: high

**Confidence and uncertainty:**

• **High confidence:** P1 classification, business impact numbers, service ownership, and runbook steps are grounded in structured data and Knowledge Base.  
• **Medium confidence:** Deployment correlation is unclear—user reports "latest deployment," but no deployments found in the past 6 hours. **Request exact deployment time and version** to strengthen correlation and rollback decision.  
• **Next step:** Confirm deployment details, then execute triage plan starting with freezing deployments and checking Redis + customer-db health.
