citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

triage_card.answer:
Priority:
P1 — 100.0% error rate indicates customer-facing outage; revenue risk $9,800/min exceeds P1 threshold; regulated markets: UKGC, DGE, MGM. Response target: 5 minutes.

Investigation findings:
Alert on bet-service in GIB-UKGC: CRITICAL: bet-service nodes unresponsive - 100% error rate on GIB-UKGC. Suspect deploy: bet-service v5.16.1 at 2026-06-10T09:55:00Z (Bet placement retry policy change - suspected P1 storm trigger). Owner: Betting Core (tier-0). Dependencies: wallet-service, auth-service, game-service, replication.

Triage plan:
1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service.
Historical: INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE: Connection pool exhaustion caused by retry storm against wallet dependency — resolved in 27 min (Gate retries with circuit breaker and alert on pool saturation before node exhaustion) | INC-2025-0001: Dependency latency — resolved in 12 min (Update runbook with validation steps) | INC-2025-0029: Insufficient retry backoff — resolved in 48 min (Add dashboard correlation panel)
2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job.
3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy.
4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes.
5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments.

Escalation recommendation:
Notify: Primary Betting Core On-Call, Secondary Betting Core On-Call, Yossi Avraham, Incident Commander, Compliance Officer Role. War room: required. Max response: 5 min.
Regulatory override (UKGC): report to UKGC Compliance Desk within 1h.

Resolution plan:
Follow the deployment rollback runbook if deploy-correlated; otherwise use the service runbook remediation.

Business impact:
P1 on bet-service: ~32,000 users affected, $9,800/min ($588,000/hr). SLA risk critical; regulatory exposure UKGC, DGE, MGM.

Sources:
service_owners.csv (bet-service); deploys.csv (GIB-UKGC); business_impact.json; priority_matrix.json; escalation_policies.json; past_incidents.csv; deployment_rollback.json

Confidence and uncertainty:
High confidence on owner, deploy correlation, and priority from structured datasets. Medium uncertainty on root cause until dependency health checks complete.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

--- doc_id: "payment_service" title: "Payment Service Context" doc_type: "service" services: "payment-service, payments-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "service ownership, payments, provider" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Payment Service Context Payment-service owns authorization, provider routing, deposit success, withdrawal processing, and payment-provider failover. ## Owner - Team: Payments - Primary escalation: Primary Payments On-Call - Secondary escalation: Secondary Payments On-Call - Channel: `#payments` ## Dependencies - External payment gateway. - Wallet service. - API Gateway. ## Business Impact Payment incidents can cause direct revenue loss, customer trust damage, and PCI-DSS/compliance follow-up.

1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with serv

triage_card.answer:
**Priority: P1 – CRITICAL**

100% error rate on bet-service in GIB-UKGC with all nodes unresponsive.

**Business Impact:**
- ~32,000 active users affected
- $9,800/minute ($588,000/hour revenue loss)
- SLA breach imminent (99.95% target)
- High regulatory exposure: UKGC, DGE, MGM
- Escalation required within 5 minutes

**Investigation Findings:**

1. **Recent Deployments:** No deployments to bet-service or dependencies (wallet-service, auth-service, game-service) in the last 6 hours – deployment correlation unlikely.

2. **Similar Historical Pattern:** INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE – same service/environment, 100% error rate caused by connection pool exhaustion from retry storm against wallet dependency. MTTR was 27 minutes via rollback + restart.

3. **Service Owner:** Betting Core team; escalation path: Primary Betting Core On-Call → Secondary Betting Core On-Call (PagerDuty service PD-BET--001).

**Triage Plan (execute in order):**

Since no recent deployment, focus on dependency/connection pool saturation:

1. **Immediate – Check wallet-service health and latency** (bet-service depends on wallet):
   - Inspect wallet-service error rate, latency p99, and health endpoints
   - Check for wallet connection timeout or throttling

2. **Check bet-service connection pool metrics:**
   - Active connections, pool saturation, retry count
   - Look for evidence of retry storm or fan-out behavior

3. **Restart unhealthy bet-service pods** after capturing logs and confirming no active schema migrations:
   - Capture logs from failing pods before restart
   - Restart saturated pods in phases to avoid thundering herd

4. **If wallet dependency is degraded:**
   - Disable retry fan-out or enable circuit breaker for wallet calls
   - Coordinate with wallet team for immediate mitigation

5. **Validate recovery:**
   - Bet placement success rate > 99%
   - Error rate < 1%
   - Connection pool utilization returns to normal

**Escalation Recommendation:**

**IMMEDIATE (within 5 minutes):**
- Notify: Primary Betting Core On-Call, Secondary Betting Core On-Call, Team Lead, Incident Commander
- Channels: PagerDuty service PD-BET--001, SNS, SES
- If wallet-service is implicated, escalate to wallet owner in parallel

**Regulatory Notification:**
- Prepare UKGC incident brief if outage exceeds 15 minutes

**Resolution Plan:**

1. Freeze further bet-service deployments until resolved
2. Primary mitigation: Restart saturated pods + disable wallet retry fan-out
3. If restart fails: Enable circuit breaker for wallet dependency
4. Monitor for 10 minutes post-mitigation:
   - Bet success rate > 99%
   - Connection pool < 80% saturation
   - No new unresponsive nodes

**Post-Incident:**
- Implement pool saturation alerting before node exhaustion
- Gate retries with circuit breaker (lesson from INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE)
- Review wallet dependency SLA and timeout configuration

**Confidence: MEDIUM**

Evidence used:
- Service context, business impact, escalation policy (structured data)
- Historical incident INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE with identical symptom + same environment (past_incidents.csv)
- Deployment rollback runbook, database connectivity runbook (KB)

**Uncertainty:**
- Root cause not yet confirmed – need real-time wallet health, connection pool metrics, and bet-service logs
- Restart may not resolve if wallet is severely degraded or if database connections are exhausted
- If wallet + pool are healthy, investigate infrastructure (network, node health, resource exhaustion)

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "redis_token_store_degradation" title: "Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "redis-token-store, auth-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "redis, session, token, auth, cache" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook Use this runbook when login succeeds intermittently, sessions disappear, token validation times out, or Redis latency/evictions rise. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check Redis `PING`, primary role, replica health, memory usage, and eviction count. 2. Compare Redis latency with auth-service token validation errors. 3. Check whether a recent auth-service deployment changed token write volume or TTL. 4. Inspect network errors between auth-service pods and Redis. 5. Confirm whether failover is active, partial, or stuck. ## Triage Decision Tree - If Redis memory is above 90% and evictions are increasing, reduce token write pressure and scale memory. - If primary is unavailable, follow the managed Redis failover process. - If only auth-service sees errors, inspect client pool size and connection reuse. ## Remediation 1. Disable noisy token refresh paths or reduce refresh frequency if introduced by deployment. 2. Scale Redis memory or shard capacity if safe and pre-approved. 3. Restart auth-service clients only after confirming Redis itself is healthy. 4. Avoid `FLUSHDB` in production unless the incident commander and Identity owner approve. ## Verification - Redis latency returns to normal. - Evictions stop increasing. - Login success and token validation rates recover. ## Escalation Escalate to Identity and Access for auth impact and Data Platform if Redis failover or capacity changes are required.

triage_card.answer:
**Priority: P1 CRITICAL**
bet-service nodes unresponsive in GIB-UKGC. Business impact: ~32,000 users affected, $9,800/min ($588,000/hr), critical SLA risk, high regulatory exposure (UKGC, DGE, MGM). This requires immediate action and escalation within 5 minutes.

**Investigation findings:**

Similar incident pattern identified: INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE was a P1 in the same environment with 100% error rate after connection-pool saturation caused by retry storm against wallet dependency. MTTR was 27 minutes. Resolution involved disabling retry fan-out, restarting bet-service pool, validating wallet latency, and monitoring bet placement success.

Historical pattern suggests bet-service node unresponsiveness is often caused by:
- Connection pool exhaustion (most common based on INC-2025-11-04)
- Dependency latency/timeouts (INC-2025-0001, INC-2025-0029)
- Recent deployments introducing retry storms or resource saturation

**Triage plan (execute in order):**

1. **Check recent deployments** - Identify if bet-service was deployed in the last 60 minutes and correlate with alert start time. Prepare rollback if deployment timing matches symptom onset.

2. **Inspect node health immediately:**
   - Validate bet-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses
   - Check connection pool saturation metrics against wallet or database dependencies
   - Monitor CPU, memory, and active connection counts
   - Review error logs for retry storms or dependency timeout patterns

3. **Check downstream dependencies:**
   - Verify wallet service latency and availability
   - Check database connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, or CPU saturation
   - Confirm payment-service and auth-service health if bet placement flow is affected

4. **Mitigation (in order of safety):**
   - If recent deployment exists and correlation is strong: freeze further releases and execute rollback
   - If retry storm detected: disable retry fan-out or apply circuit breaker
   - If dependency saturation: reduce bet-service replica count to relieve downstream pressure
   - Only restart unhealthy pods after capturing diagnostic logs

5. **Avoid:** Do not kill database sessions, flush cache stores, or restart all nodes simultaneously without evidence and approval.

**Escalation recommendation:**

**IMMEDIATE:** Escalate to Primary Betting Core On-Call via PD-BET-001 (Secondary Betting Core On-Call as backup). Owner team: Betting Core.

This is P1 with critical regulatory exposure (UKGC) and $9,800/min revenue impact. Escalation must occur within 5 minutes per policy. Notify incident commander with:
- Service: bet-service
- Environment: GIB-UKGC  
- Symptom: Nodes unresponsive
- Business impact: 32K users, $588K/hr
- Deployment correlation status (pending check)
- Rollback readiness

**Resolution validation:**

After mitigation:
- Error rate and node response time return to baseline for at least 10 minutes
- Connection pool saturation clears
- Bet placement success rate recovers to SLA target (99.95% uptime)
- No new alerts fire for bet-service
- Confirm prior version in deployment inventory if rollback was executed
- Monitor wallet and database health for cascading recovery

Post-incident: Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis, circuit breaker validation, and pre-deploy connection pool checks.

**Sources:**
- Deployment Rollback Runbook [9V0Q]
- Historical Deployment Incidents (INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE) [7yN2]
- Database Connectivity Runbook [dnnk]
- Past incidents database (5 similar incidents)
- Service context and business impact data

**Confidence: MEDIUM** - Strong historical pattern match and business impact data available. Need deployment timeline and real-time pod health metrics to confirm root cause. Rollback readiness unknown without deployment verification.

**Immediate next steps:**
1. Provide alert timestamp for deployment correlation
2. Check bet-service pod status and connection pool metrics NOW
3. Escalate to Betting Core On-Call immediately - do not wait for full RCA

MTTR was 27 minutes. Resolution involved disabling retry fan-out, restarting bet-service pool, validating wallet latency, and monitoring bet placement success. Historical pattern suggests bet-service node unresponsiveness is often caused by: - Connection pool exhaustion (most common based on INC-2025-11-04) - Dependency latency/timeouts (INC-2025-0001, INC-2025-0029) - Recent deployments introducing retry storms or resource saturation **Triage plan (execute in order):**
**Check recent deployments** - Identify if bet-service was deployed in the last 60 minutes and correlate with alert start time. Prepare rollback if deployment timing matches symptom onset.

**Inspect node health immediately:** - Validate bet-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses - Check connection pool saturation metrics against wallet or database dependencies - Monitor CPU, memory, and active connection counts - Review error logs for retry storms or dependency timeout patterns

**Check downstream dependencies:** - Verify wallet service latency and availability - Check database connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, or CPU saturation - Confirm payment-service and auth-service health if bet placement flow is affected

**Mitigation (in order of safety):** - If recent deployment exists and correlation is strong: freeze further releases and execute rollback - If retry storm detected: disable retry fan-out or apply circuit breaker - If dependency saturation: reduce bet-service replica count to relieve downstream pressure - Only restart unhealthy pods after capturing diagnostic logs

**Avoid:** Do not kill database sessions, flush cache stores, or restart all nodes simultaneously without evidence and approval. **Escalation recommendation:** **IMMEDIATE:** Escalate to Primary Betting Core On-Call via PD-BET-001 (Secondary Betting Core On-Call as backup). Owner team: Betting Core. This is P1 with critical regulatory exposure (UKGC) and $9,800/min revenue impact. Escalation must occur within 5 minutes per policy. Notify incident commander with: - Service: bet-service - Environment: GIB-UKGC - Symptom: Nodes unresponsive - Business impact: 32K users, $588K/hr - Deployment correlation status (pending check) - Rollback readiness **Resolution validation:** After mitigation: - Error rate and node response time return to baseline for at least 10 minutes - Connection pool saturation clears - Bet placement success rate recovers to SLA target (99.95% uptime) - No new alerts fire for bet-service - Confirm prior version in deployment inventory if rollback was executed - Monitor wallet and database health for cascading recovery Post-incident: Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis, circuit breaker validation, and pre-deploy connection pool checks. **Sources:** - Deployment Rollback Runbook [9V0Q] - Historical Deployment Incidents (INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE) [7yN2] - Database Connectivity Runbook [dnnk] - Past incidents database (5 similar incidents) - Service context and business impact data **Confidence: MEDIUM** - Strong historical pattern match and business impact data available. Need deployment timeline and real-time pod health metrics to confirm root cause. Rollback readiness unknown without deployment verification. **Immediate next steps:**
Provide alert timestamp for deployment correlation
Check bet-service pod status and connection pool metrics NOW
Escalate to Betting Core On-Call immediately - do not wait for full RCA

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.

--- doc_id: "historical_auth_incidents" title: "Historical Auth Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "historical, auth, login, redis, deployment" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Auth Incidents ## INC-AUTH-2026-03-08 Auth-service latency and login timeouts increased after a client-pool change. Root cause was connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store. Resolution was rollback plus lower token refresh concurrency. Lesson: after auth deployments, check Redis latency and pool saturation before changing database settings. ## INC-AUTH-2025-10-28 Login failures affected EU traffic after CDN and gateway configuration drift. Root cause was an invalid route rule for OIDC callback paths. Resolution was gateway route rollback and drift detection. ## Reuse Guidance When a new incident mentions users cannot log in after latest deployment, first compare deployment time, Redis token health, customer-db health, and API Gateway routes.

--- doc_id: "redis_token_store_degradation" title: "Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "redis-token-store, auth-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "redis, session, token, auth, cache" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Redis Token Store Degradation Runbook Use this runbook when login succeeds intermittently, sessions disappear, token validation times out, or Redis latency/evictions rise. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check Redis `PING`, primary role, replica health, memory usage, and eviction count. 2. Compare Redis latency with auth-service token validation errors. 3. Check whether a recent auth-service deployment changed token write volume or TTL. 4. Inspect network errors between auth-service pods and Redis. 5. Confirm whether failover is active, partial, or stuck. ## Triage Decision Tree - If Redis memory is above 90% and evictions are increasing, reduce token write pressure and scale memory. - If primary is unavailable, follow the managed Redis failover process. - If only auth-service sees errors, inspect client pool size and connection reuse. ## Remediation 1. Disable noisy token refresh paths or reduce refresh frequency if introduced by deployment. 2. Scale Redis memory or shard capacity if safe and pre-approved. 3. Restart auth-service clients only after confirming Redis itself is healthy. 4. Avoid `FLUSHDB` in production unless the incident commander and Identity owner approve. ## Verification - Redis latency returns to normal. - Evictions stop increasing. - Login success and token validation rates recover. ## Escalation Escalate to Identity and Access for auth impact and Data Platform if Redis failover or capacity changes are required.

knowledge_base.triage_decision_tree:
- Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available.
- Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected.
- Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present.
triage_steps: Freeze further deployments for the affected service.
Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job.
Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy.
Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes.
Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments.
knowledge_base.verification: - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory.
knowledge_base.escalation: For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

triage_card.answer:
I need the alert timestamp to check for recent deployments. Let me ask for that while proceeding with initial triage:

**Priority: P1 CRITICAL**

**Investigation findings:**
- **Service Context:** bet-service (Betting Core team) in GIB-UKGC environment
- **Business Impact:** ~32,000 active users affected, **$9,800/min loss ($588,000/hr)**. Critical SLA risk with **high regulatory exposure** (UKGC, DGE, MGM)
- **Similar Incident:** INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE - bet-service 100% error rate from connection pool exhaustion caused by retry storm against wallet dependency (MTTR: 27 min, resolved via retry disablement + pod restart)
- **Pattern Match:** Unresponsive nodes often indicate connection pool saturation, retry storms, or cascading dependency failures

**Triage plan (ordered, reversible steps):**

1. **Check deployment correlation** - Need alert timestamp to query recent deploys within 60-min window
2. **Inspect bet-service pod health** - Check readiness probes, pod restarts, and /health endpoints
3. **Check connection pool saturation** - Monitor pool metrics to wallet-service and database dependencies
4. **Identify retry storm** - Look for exponential retry fan-out against downstream services (wallet, payment)
5. **Check dependency health** - Validate wallet-service and database latency/availability
6. **Prepare rollback** - If deployment correlation is strong (<60 min), prepare immediate rollback
7. **Circuit breaker validation** - Confirm retry circuit breakers are functioning

**⚠️ NEED: What is the alert timestamp (ISO-8601 format, e.g., 2026-06-10T09:00:00Z)? This is required to correlate with recent deployments.**

**Escalation recommendation:**
- **IMMEDIATE:** Notify Primary Betting Core On-Call (PagerDuty: PD-BET-001) - P1 with $9,800/min impact and regulatory exposure requires 5-minute escalation window
- **Incident Commander:** Required for P1 with multi-regulator exposure

**Resolution plan:**
Based on historical pattern (INC-2025-11-04):
1. Disable retry fan-out or feature flags affecting retry behavior
2. Restart saturated bet-service pods after capturing diagnostic logs
3. Validate wallet-service latency returns to baseline
4. Monitor bet placement success rate for 10 minutes
5. If rollback deployed, confirm prior version in inventory

**Sources:**
- Service owner/impact: <REDACTED> (business_impact.json, service_owners.csv)
- Similar incident: INC-2025-11-04-GIB-BET-OUTAGE (past_incidents.csv)
- Runbook guidance: deployment_rollback.json [k8Yp], database_connectivity [8sLq]

**Confidence: MEDIUM** - Strong pattern match to prior connection pool exhaustion incident, but need deployment correlation to confirm root cause. High confidence on business impact and escalation path.

**Provide alert timestamp to proceed with deployment correlation analysis.**

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "api_gateway_5xx" title: "API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "api-gateway, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "api-gateway, 5xx, ingress, routing, timeout" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # API Gateway 5xx Troubleshooting Runbook Use this runbook when API Gateway returns 5xx, 502, 503, or 504 errors for production traffic. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify failing route, stage, integration target, and market. 2. Compare API Gateway 5xx with backend service 5xx, latency, and health checks. 3. Check recent deployments to API Gateway routing, auth-service, and upstream services. 4. Inspect integration timeout, throttling, WAF blocks, and certificate errors. 5. Validate whether public synthetic checks fail from multiple regions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If backend errors mirror gateway errors, follow the backend service runbook. - If backend is healthy but gateway fails, inspect route configuration and integration timeout. - If only authenticated routes fail, check auth-service and token validation. ## Remediation 1. Roll back recent gateway route or stage configuration changes. 2. Shift traffic away from a failing integration target if supported. 3. Increase backend capacity only after confirming saturation is the cause. 4. Avoid deleting stages or custom domain mappings during an incident. ## Verification - Gateway 5xx drops below 1%. - p99 latency returns to normal. - Synthetic checks pass for authenticated and anonymous routes. ## Escalation Escalate to Edge Platform for gateway configuration or WAF issues, and to the backend owner when integration errors mirror service errors.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

--- doc_id: "database_connectivity" title: "Database Connectivity Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "customer-db, postgres, auth-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "database, postgres, connectivity, cpu, pool, locks" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Database Connectivity Runbook Use this runbook for PostgreSQL CPU saturation, connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, replica lag, or auth-service customer-db errors. ## Investigation Steps 1. Check database CPU, active connections, connection pool saturation, lock waits, and slow queries. 2. Compare database symptoms with the affected service deployment timeline. 3. Identify whether failures are reads, writes, or authentication-specific lookups. 4. Check replica lag before shifting read traffic. 5. Inspect the top queries and recent migrations for missing indexes or long transactions. ## Triage Decision Tree - If CPU is high with one dominant query, mitigate the query or roll back the service that introduced it. - If connections are exhausted, reduce client pool pressure before killing database sessions. - If a migration lock is blocking auth-service reads or writes, involve DBA and service owner immediately. - If replica lag affects reads only, remove the lagging replica from rotation. ## Remediation 1. Stop the newest deployment or feature flag that created the load. 2. Reduce service replica count or connection pool size if the database is overloaded by clients. 3. Kill only confirmed blocking sessions with DBA approval. 4. Roll back a schema or application change only when the rollback plan is verified. ## Verification - CPU remains below alert threshold for 10 minutes. - Connection pool saturation clears. - Auth-service login checks pass. - No new lock waits accumulate. ## Escalation Escalate to Platform DBA for P1/P2 database incidents. Include service, query evidence, deployment correlation, business impact, and rollback status.
