citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

answer_sections.summary: P1. Bet-service node unresponsiveness directly impacts customer betting operations and revenue. Production service outage with immediate customer-facing impact and SLA breach risk. Investigation findings: Historical evidence shows bet-service P1 incidents have followed deployment changes, specifically a retry-policy deployment that caused connection pool saturation and pod unresponsiveness. The pattern indicates that recent deployments to bet-service are a primary suspect. Node unresponsiveness typically stems from resource exhaustion (connection pools, memory, CPU) or pod health probe failures rather than network isolation. Triage plan: 1. Confirm the exact time nodes became unresponsive and cross-reference with any bet-service production deployments in the last 60 minutes. 2. Check bet-service pod health status, readiness probe responses, and restart counts. 3. Inspect connection pool saturation, memory pressure, and CPU utilization on affected nodes. 4. Review recent deployment changes, feature flags, and retry policy or concurrency settings. 5. Validate downstream dependency health (databases, caches, API Gateway) for cascading failures. 6. If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as primary remediation. Escalation recommendation: Escalate immediately to the bet-service owner and incident commander. If a deployment occurred within the last hour, also notify the platform release owner. This is a P1 production outage requiring urgent coordination for rollback decision and execution. Resolution plan: 1. If deployment is within 60 minutes of unresponsiveness start and rollback is available, execute rollback immediately while continuing diagnostics. 2. Freeze further bet-service deployments until incident is resolved. 3. Monitor pod rollout status and readiness probes during recovery. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics (bet placement latency, error rate) return to baseline for at least 10 minutes. 5. Capture logs from affected pods before restart if unresponsiveness persists after rollback. 6. Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis of the failed release. Business impact: Bet-service outage blocks all customer betting transactions, directly impacting revenue and customer trust. SLA breach is imminent. Historical similar incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) was classified P1 and required urgent rollback. Confidence and uncertainty: Confidence: Medium-High. Historical pattern strongly suggests deployment-triggered connection pool or resource saturation. However, search results do not contain current deployment logs, pod metrics, or alert timestamps. Missing data: exact time of unresponsiveness, recent deployment details, current pod health status, connection pool metrics, and whether this affects all nodes or a subset. Obtain these immediately to confirm rollback decision.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

triage_card.answer:
Priority: P1

Rationale: Bet-service is a tier-0 revenue-blocking service in the GIB-UKGC regulated environment. 100% error rate means all customer betting is blocked, creating immediate revenue impact and regulatory compliance risk. This meets P1 criteria for production outage affecting core business function. Investigation findings:

Historical evidence shows a similar P1 incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) where bet-service in GIB-UKGC experienced 100% errors following a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. This pattern is directly relevant to the current incident.

The deployment rollback runbook indicates that when a service becomes unresponsive soon after a release, rollback is the primary remediation path. The decision tree states: roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available.

Key checks needed:
- Confirm latest bet-service deployment time and correlation with error start time
- Verify rollback availability and prior version status
- Check for connection pool saturation, retry loops, or circuit breaker issues
- Validate database migration compatibility if schema changes are present Triage plan:

1. Identify the latest production deployment for bet-service and compare deployment timestamp with alert start time. If within 60 minutes, prepare rollback.

2. Check bet-service pod health, readiness probes, and resource saturation (CPU, memory, connection pools).

3. Validate downstream dependencies: confirm database connectivity, connection pool exhaustion, and any recent schema migrations.

4. Review recent feature flags or configuration changes that may have altered retry behavior or connection handling.

5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service name (bet-service), affected environment (GIB-UKGC), version, and expected recovery time.

6. Freeze further deployments for bet-service until incident commander approval.

7. Execute rollback command or CI/CD rollback job and monitor pod rollout status until all nodes are healthy.

8. Validate error rate and latency return to baseline for at least 10 minutes before declaring recovery. Escalation recommendation:

Escalate immediately to:
- Bet-service owner
- Incident commander
- Platform release owner

This is a P1 production outage in a regulated environment (GIB-UKGC) with 100% error rate. Escalation is required per the deployment rollback runbook for P1 incidents. Do not wait for manual investigation to complete; initiate rollback preparation in parallel with escalation. Resolution plan:

1. Execute approved rollback command to revert bet-service to the prior stable version.

2. Monitor rollout status until all pods transition to healthy state and readiness probes pass.

3. Validate that error rate drops to zero and latency returns to baseline within 10 minutes.

4. Confirm no new alerts fire for bet-service after rollback completes.

5. Verify the previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory.

6. Open a follow-up incident ticket for the failed release to prevent redeployment of the same code.

7. Unfreeze deployments only after incident commander approval and root-cause analysis begins.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "business_impact_guide" title: "Business Impact Interpretation" doc_type: "guide" services: "all" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE, MGM, MIRAGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3,P4" tags: "business_impact, tier, revenue, compliance" last_updated: "2026-06-09" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- Use the piter-service-context Action Group and data/source/business_impact.json for numeric impact scores, tier labels, and environment-specific revenue exposure. ## How to explain impact - Tier-0 services (auth-service, wallet-service, bet-service, payments-service, replication) block revenue or regulated play when degraded in production. - Auth-service login failures in GIB-UKGC affect regulated UKGC traffic; cite tier-0 and login dependency on Redis token store. - Always pair narrative impact with severity (P1-P4) and environment code from the alert. Do not invent dollar amounts; quote Action Group fields or say data is unavailable.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

--- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

triage_card.answer:
Priority:
P1. Bet-service node unresponsiveness directly impacts customer betting operations and revenue. Production service outage with immediate customer-facing impact and SLA breach risk. Investigation findings:
Historical evidence shows bet-service P1 incidents have followed deployment changes, specifically a retry-policy deployment that caused connection pool saturation and pod unresponsiveness. The pattern indicates that recent deployments to bet-service are a primary suspect. Node unresponsiveness typically stems from resource exhaustion (connection pools, memory, CPU) or pod health probe failures rather than network isolation. Triage plan:
1. Confirm the exact time nodes became unresponsive and cross-reference with any bet-service production deployments in the last 60 minutes.
2. Check bet-service pod health status, readiness probe responses, and restart counts.
3. Inspect connection pool saturation, memory pressure, and CPU utilization on affected nodes.
4. Review recent deployment changes, feature flags, and retry policy or concurrency settings.
5. Validate downstream dependency health (databases, caches, API Gateway) for cascading failures.
6. If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as primary remediation. Escalation recommendation:
Escalate immediately to the bet-service owner and incident commander. If a deployment occurred within the last hour, also notify the platform release owner. This is a P1 production outage requiring urgent coordination for rollback decision and execution. Resolution plan:
1. If deployment is within 60 minutes of unresponsiveness start and rollback is available, execute rollback immediately while continuing diagnostics.
2. Freeze further bet-service deployments until incident is resolved.
3. Monitor pod rollout status and readiness probes during recovery.
4. Validate customer-facing success metrics (bet placement latency, error rate) return to baseline for at least 10 minutes.
5. Capture logs from affected pods before restart if unresponsiveness persists after rollback.
6. Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis of the failed release. Business impact:
Bet-service outage blocks all customer betting transactions, directly impacting revenue and customer trust. SLA breach is imminent. Historical similar incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) was classified P1 and required urgent rollback. Confidence and uncertainty:
Confidence: Medium-High. Historical pattern strongly suggests deployment-triggered connection pool or resource saturation. However, search results do not contain current deployment logs, pod metrics, or alert timestamps. Missing data: exact time of unresponsiveness, recent deployment details, current pod health status, connection pool metrics, and whether this affects all nodes or a subset. Obtain these immediately to confirm rollback decision.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

triage_card.answer:
Priority: P1

Rationale: Bet-service is a tier-0 revenue-blocking service in the GIB-UKGC regulated environment. 100% error rate means all customer betting is blocked, creating immediate revenue impact and regulatory compliance risk. This meets P1 criteria for production outage affecting core business function. Investigation findings:

Historical evidence shows a similar P1 incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) where bet-service in GIB-UKGC experienced 100% errors following a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. This pattern is directly relevant to the current incident.

The deployment rollback runbook indicates that when a service becomes unresponsive soon after a release, rollback is the primary remediation path. The decision tree states: roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available.

Key checks needed:
- Confirm latest bet-service deployment time and correlation with error start time
- Verify rollback availability and prior version status
- Check for connection pool saturation, retry loops, or circuit breaker issues
- Validate database connectivity and dependency health Triage plan:

1. Identify the latest bet-service production deployment for GIB-UKGC and compare deployment timestamp with alert start time. If within 60 minutes, prepare rollback.

2. Check bet-service pod health, readiness probes, and error logs for connection pool exhaustion, retry fan-out, or circuit breaker state.

3. Validate downstream dependencies: database connectivity, connection pool saturation, and any recent configuration changes.

4. Confirm rollback path is available and prior version is known in deployment inventory.

5. Announce rollback intent in incident channel with service name, affected version, and expected recovery time.

6. Execute rollback command via CI/CD pipeline and monitor pod rollout status until all nodes are healthy.

7. Validate error rate returns to baseline and customer-facing metrics recover for at least 10 minutes before unfreezing deployments. Escalation recommendation:

Escalate immediately to:
- Bet-service owner (for rollback approval and post-incident investigation)
- Incident commander (to coordinate P1 response)
- Platform release owner (to freeze further deployments and manage rollback execution)

Escalate now because this is a P1 production outage with 100% error rate blocking all revenue in a regulated market. Do not wait for manual investigation to complete; initiate rollback preparation in parallel. Resolution plan:

1. Freeze all further bet-service deployments immediately.

2. Execute the approved rollback command to revert to the prior stable version.

3. Monitor rollout status in real-time until all bet-service pods report healthy and ready.

4. Validate that error rate drops to baseline (near 0%) and latency returns to normal within 10 minutes.

5. Confirm no new alerts fire for bet-service after rollback completes.

6. Verify the previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory.

7. Open a follow-up incident ticket for root-cause analysis of the failed release (connection pool, retry policy, or other saturation cause).

8. Unfreeze deployments only after incident commander approval and follow-up ticket is created.

Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

triage_card.answer:
Priority:
P1. Bet-service node unresponsiveness directly impacts customer betting operations and revenue. Production service outage with immediate customer-facing impact and SLA breach risk. Investigation findings:
Historical evidence shows bet-service P1 incidents have followed deployment changes, specifically a retry-policy deployment that caused connection pool saturation and pod unresponsiveness. The pattern indicates that recent deployments to bet-service are a primary suspect. Node unresponsiveness typically stems from resource exhaustion (connection pools, memory, CPU) or pod health probe failures rather than network isolation. Triage plan:
1. Confirm the exact time nodes became unresponsive and cross-reference with any bet-service production deployments in the last 60 minutes.
2. Check bet-service pod health status, readiness probe responses, and restart counts.
3. Inspect connection pool saturation, memory pressure, and CPU utilization on affected nodes.
4. Review recent deployment changes, feature flags, and retry policy or concurrency settings.
5. Validate downstream dependency health (databases, caches, API Gateway) for cascading failures.
6. If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as primary remediation. Escalation recommendation:
Escalate immediately to the bet-service owner and incident commander. If a deployment occurred within the last hour, also notify the platform release owner. This is a P1 production outage requiring urgent coordination for rollback decision and execution. Resolution plan:
1. If deployment is within 60 minutes of unresponsiveness start and rollback is available, execute rollback immediately while continuing diagnostics.
2. Freeze further bet-service deployments until incident is resolved.
3. Monitor pod rollout status and readiness probes during recovery.
4. Validate customer-facing success metrics (bet placement latency, error rate) return to baseline for at least 10 minutes.
5. Capture logs from affected pods before restart if unresponsiveness persists after rollback.
6. Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis of the failed release. Business impact:
Bet-service outage blocks all customer betting transactions, directly impacting revenue and customer trust. SLA breach is imminent. Historical similar incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) was classified P1 and required urgent rollback. Confidence and uncertainty:
Confidence: Medium-High. Historical pattern strongly suggests deployment-triggered connection pool or resource saturation. However, search results do not contain current deployment logs, pod metrics, or alert timestamps. Missing data: exact time of unresponsiveness, recent deployment details, current pod health status, connection pool metrics, and whether this affects all nodes or a subset. Obtain these immediately to confirm rollback decision.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

triage_card.answer:
Priority:
P1. Bet-service node unresponsiveness directly impacts customer betting operations and revenue. Production service outage with immediate customer-facing impact and SLA breach risk. Investigation findings:
Historical evidence shows bet-service P1 incidents have followed deployment changes, specifically a retry-policy deployment that caused connection pool saturation and pod unresponsiveness. The pattern indicates that recent deployments to bet-service are a primary suspect. Node unresponsiveness typically stems from resource exhaustion (connection pools, memory, CPU) or pod health probe failures rather than network isolation. Triage plan:
1. Confirm the exact time nodes became unresponsive and cross-reference with any bet-service production deployments in the last 60 minutes.
2. Check bet-service pod health status, readiness probe responses, and restart counts.
3. Inspect connection pool saturation, memory pressure, and CPU utilization on affected nodes.
4. Review recent deployment changes, feature flags, and retry policy or concurrency settings.
5. Validate downstream dependency health (databases, caches, API Gateway) for cascading failures.
6. If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as primary remediation. Escalation recommendation:
Escalate immediately to the bet-service owner and incident commander. If a deployment occurred within the last hour, also notify the platform release owner. This is a P1 production outage requiring urgent coordination for rollback decision and execution. Resolution plan:
1. If deployment is within 60 minutes of unresponsiveness start and rollback is available, execute rollback immediately while continuing diagnostics.
2. Freeze further bet-service deployments until incident is resolved.
3. Monitor pod rollout status and readiness probes during recovery.
4. Validate customer-facing success metrics (bet placement latency, error rate) return to baseline for at least 10 minutes.
5. Capture logs from affected pods before restart if unresponsiveness persists after rollback.
6. Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis of the failed release. Business impact:
Bet-service outage blocks all customer betting transactions, directly impacting revenue and customer trust. SLA breach is imminent. Historical similar incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) was classified P1 and required urgent rollback. Confidence and uncertainty:
Confidence: Medium-High. Historical pattern strongly suggests deployment-triggered connection pool or resource saturation. However, search results do not contain current deployment logs, pod metrics, or alert timestamps. Missing data: exact time of unresponsiveness, recent deployment details, current pod health status, connection pool metrics, and whether this affects all nodes or a subset. Obtain these immediately to confirm rollback decision.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

triage_card.answer:
Priority:
P1. Bet-service node unresponsiveness directly impacts customer betting operations and revenue. Production service outage with immediate customer-facing impact and SLA breach risk. Investigation findings:
Historical evidence shows bet-service P1 incidents have followed deployment changes, specifically a retry-policy deployment that caused connection pool saturation and pod unresponsiveness. The pattern indicates that recent deployments to bet-service are a primary suspect. Node unresponsiveness typically stems from resource exhaustion (connection pools, memory, CPU) or pod health probe failures rather than network isolation. Triage plan:
1. Confirm the exact time nodes became unresponsive and cross-reference with any bet-service production deployments in the last 60 minutes.
2. Check bet-service pod health status, readiness probe responses, and restart counts.
3. Inspect connection pool saturation, memory pressure, and CPU utilization on affected nodes.
4. Review recent deployment changes, feature flags, and retry policy or concurrency settings.
5. Validate downstream dependency health (databases, caches, API Gateway) for cascading failures.
6. If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as primary remediation. Escalation recommendation:
Escalate immediately to the bet-service owner and incident commander. If a deployment occurred within the last hour, also notify the platform release owner. This is a P1 production outage requiring urgent coordination for rollback decision and execution. Resolution plan:
1. If deployment is within 60 minutes of unresponsiveness start and rollback is available, execute rollback immediately while continuing diagnostics.
2. Freeze further bet-service deployments until incident is resolved.
3. Monitor pod rollout status and readiness probes during recovery.
4. Validate customer-facing success metrics (bet placement latency, error rate) return to baseline for at least 10 minutes.
5. Capture logs from affected pods before restart if unresponsiveness persists after rollback.
6. Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis of the failed release. Business impact:
Bet-service outage blocks all customer betting transactions, directly impacting revenue and customer trust. SLA breach is imminent. Historical similar incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) was classified P1 and required urgent rollback. Confidence and uncertainty:
Confidence: Medium-High. Historical pattern strongly suggests deployment-triggered connection pool or resource saturation. However, search results do not contain current deployment logs, pod metrics, or alert timestamps. Missing data: exact time of unresponsiveness, recent deployment details, current pod health status, connection pool metrics, and whether this affects all nodes or a subset. Obtain these immediately to confirm rollback decision.

--- doc_id: "auth_service_login_failure" title: "Auth Service Login Failure Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, redis-token-store, customer-db" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3" tags: "auth, login, deployment, tokens, session, outage" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Auth Service Login Failure Runbook Use this runbook when users cannot log in, token validation fails, MFA loops, or auth-service error rate increases after a production deployment. ## Investigation Steps 1. Confirm the production error rate, affected markets, and whether login failures started within 60 minutes of a deployment. 2. Check recent auth-service deployments, feature flags, schema migrations, and token-signing key changes. 3. Validate auth-service pod health, readiness probes, and `/health/ready` responses. 4. Check Redis token store latency, evictions, memory pressure, and primary/replica status. 5. Check customer-db connectivity for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, and migration locks. 6. Confirm OIDC discovery and public API Gateway routes are returning valid responses. ## Triage Decision Tree - If the latest deployment is within the last hour and failures started immediately after it, prepare rollback first. - If login returns 401 for valid users and Redis has evictions or high latency, follow the Redis token store runbook. - If auth-service pods are healthy but API Gateway returns 5xx, follow the API Gateway 5xx runbook. - If customer-db has lock waits or exhausted connections, follow the database connectivity runbook. ## Remediation 1. Freeze additional auth-service releases until the incident commander approves. 2. Roll back the latest auth-service deployment if deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available. 3. Disable newly released feature flags for token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. 4. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after capturing logs. 5.

triage_card.answer:
Priority: P1

This is a production authentication outage affecting user login immediately after deployment. Login failures block all customer access and represent maximum business impact. This meets P1 criteria for customer-facing service degradation with immediate SLA risk. Investigation findings:

The evidence pattern matches known auth-service post-deployment failures. Historical incidents show that login failures after deployment typically stem from: connection pool exhaustion between auth-service and Redis token store, token-signing key changes, schema migrations, or API Gateway route configuration drift. The timing correlation (failure within 60 minutes of deployment) is the strongest diagnostic signal. Similar incidents have involved Redis evictions, high latency, customer-db lock waits, or invalid OIDC callback routes. Triage plan:

1. Confirm error rate spike and affected geographic markets; verify login failures started within 60 minutes of the latest auth-service deployment.
2. Review the deployment changelog for feature flags, token validation changes, MFA routing changes, schema migrations, or token-signing key updates.
3. Check auth-service pod health and readiness probe responses at `/health/ready`.
4. Measure Redis token store latency, memory evictions, and primary/replica replication status.
5. Check customer-db for connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, or active migration locks.
6. Validate API Gateway routes and OIDC discovery endpoints are returning valid responses.
7. Capture logs from unhealthy auth-service pods before any restart. Escalation recommendation:

Escalate immediately to the Identity and Access primary on-call engineer. If no acknowledgement within the escalation policy window, escalate to the secondary on-call. This is a P1 login outage requiring immediate senior response. Do not wait for triage completion before escalating. Resolution plan:

If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as the primary recovery path. In parallel: disable any newly released feature flags affecting token validation, MFA routing, or session write behavior. Restart only unhealthy auth-service pods after log capture. Validate recovery by confirming auth_login_success_rate returns to baseline, auth_login_error_rate stays below 1% for 10 minutes, Redis token reads/writes stabilize, and synthetic login tests pass in production before declaring mitigation complete. Business impact:

Production login outage blocks all customer access and revenue-generating transactions. SLA breach is imminent. Customer trust is at maximum risk. Every minute of outage increases financial and reputational damage. Confidence and uncertainty:

Confidence is high that this is a post-deployment auth-service issue based on timing and symptom match to historical patterns. However, the specific root cause (Redis pool exhaustion, token key change, gateway route drift, or database connectivity) cannot be determined without live system data. Missing evidence: current error logs, deployment diff, Redis metrics, customer-db connection pool status, and API Gateway route validation. Proceed immediately with triage steps 1–6 to narrow the root cause.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "business_impact_guide" title: "Business Impact Interpretation" doc_type: "guide" services: "all" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE, MGM, MIRAGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3,P4" tags: "business_impact, tier, revenue, compliance" last_updated: "2026-06-09" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- Use the piter-service-context Action Group and data/source/business_impact.json for numeric impact scores, tier labels, and environment-specific revenue exposure. ## How to explain impact - Tier-0 services (auth-service, wallet-service, bet-service, payments-service, replication) block revenue or regulated play when degraded in production. - Auth-service login failures in GIB-UKGC affect regulated UKGC traffic; cite tier-0 and login dependency on Redis token store. - Always pair narrative impact with severity (P1-P4) and environment code from the alert. Do not invent dollar amounts; quote Action Group fields or say data is unavailable.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

- Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected.
- Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present.
triage_steps: Freeze further deployments for the affected service.
Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job.
Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy.
Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes.
Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments.
knowledge_base.verification: - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory.
knowledge_base.escalation: For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

triage_card.answer:
Priority: P1

Rationale: Bet-service is a tier-0 revenue-blocking service in the GIB-UKGC regulated environment. 100% error rate means all customer betting is blocked, creating immediate revenue impact and regulatory compliance risk. This meets P1 criteria for production outage affecting core business function. Investigation findings:

Historical evidence shows a similar P1 incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) where bet-service in GIB-UKGC experienced 100% errors following a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. This pattern is directly relevant to the current incident.

The deployment rollback runbook indicates that when a service becomes unresponsive soon after a release, rollback is the primary remediation path. The decision tree states: roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available.

Key checks needed:
- Confirm latest bet-service deployment time and correlation with error start time
- Verify rollback availability and prior version status
- Check for connection pool saturation, retry loops, or circuit breaker issues
- Validate database migration compatibility if schema changes are present Triage plan:

1. Identify the latest production deployment for bet-service and compare deployment timestamp with alert start time. If within 60 minutes, prepare rollback.

2. Check bet-service pod health, readiness probes, and resource saturation (CPU, memory, connection pools).

3. Validate downstream dependencies: confirm database connectivity, connection pool exhaustion, and any recent schema migrations.

4. Review recent feature flags or configuration changes that may have altered retry behavior or connection handling.

5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service name (bet-service), affected environment (GIB-UKGC), version, and expected recovery time.

6. Freeze further deployments for bet-service until incident commander approval.

7. Execute rollback command or CI/CD rollback job and monitor pod rollout status until all nodes are healthy.

8. Validate error rate and latency return to baseline for at least 10 minutes before declaring recovery. Escalation recommendation:

Escalate immediately to:
- Bet-service owner
- Incident commander
- Platform release owner

This is a P1 production outage in a regulated environment (GIB-UKGC) with 100% error rate. Escalation is required per the deployment rollback runbook for P1 incidents. Do not wait for manual investigation to complete; initiate rollback preparation in parallel with escalation. Resolution plan:

1. Execute approved rollback command to revert bet-service to the prior stable version.

2. Monitor rollout status until all pods transition to healthy state and readiness probes pass.

3. Validate that error rate drops to zero and latency returns to baseline within 10 minutes.

4. Confirm no new alerts fire for bet-service after rollback completes.

5. Verify the previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory.

6. Open a follow-up incident ticket for the failed release to prevent redeployment of the same code.

7. Unfreeze deployments only after incident commander approval and root-cause analysis begins.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "business_impact_guide" title: "Business Impact Interpretation" doc_type: "guide" services: "all" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE, MGM, MIRAGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2,P3,P4" tags: "business_impact, tier, revenue, compliance" last_updated: "2026-06-09" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- Use the piter-service-context Action Group and data/source/business_impact.json for numeric impact scores, tier labels, and environment-specific revenue exposure. ## How to explain impact - Tier-0 services (auth-service, wallet-service, bet-service, payments-service, replication) block revenue or regulated play when degraded in production. - Auth-service login failures in GIB-UKGC affect regulated UKGC traffic; cite tier-0 and login dependency on Redis token store. - Always pair narrative impact with severity (P1-P4) and environment code from the alert. Do not invent dollar amounts; quote Action Group fields or say data is unavailable.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.

Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

triage_card.answer:
Priority:
P1. Bet-service node unresponsiveness directly impacts customer betting operations and revenue. Production service outage with immediate customer-facing impact and SLA breach risk. Investigation findings:
Historical evidence shows bet-service P1 incidents have followed deployment changes, specifically a retry-policy deployment that caused connection pool saturation and pod unresponsiveness. The pattern indicates that recent deployments to bet-service are a primary suspect. Node unresponsiveness typically stems from resource exhaustion (connection pools, memory, CPU) or pod health probe failures rather than network isolation. Triage plan:
1. Confirm the exact time nodes became unresponsive and cross-reference with any bet-service production deployments in the last 60 minutes.
2. Check bet-service pod health status, readiness probe responses, and restart counts.
3. Inspect connection pool saturation, memory pressure, and CPU utilization on affected nodes.
4. Review recent deployment changes, feature flags, and retry policy or concurrency settings.
5. Validate downstream dependency health (databases, caches, API Gateway) for cascading failures.
6. If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as primary remediation. Escalation recommendation:
Escalate immediately to the bet-service owner and incident commander. If a deployment occurred within the last hour, also notify the platform release owner. This is a P1 production outage requiring urgent coordination for rollback decision and execution. Resolution plan:
1. If deployment is within 60 minutes of unresponsiveness start and rollback is available, execute rollback immediately while continuing diagnostics.
2. Freeze further bet-service deployments until incident is resolved.
3. Monitor pod rollout status and readiness probes during recovery.
4. Validate customer-facing success metrics (bet placement latency, error rate) return to baseline for at least 10 minutes.
5. Capture logs from affected pods before restart if unresponsiveness persists after rollback.
6. Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis of the failed release. Business impact:
Bet-service outage blocks all customer betting transactions, directly impacting revenue and customer trust. SLA breach is imminent. Historical similar incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) was classified P1 and required urgent rollback. Confidence and uncertainty:
Confidence: Medium-High. Historical pattern strongly suggests deployment-triggered connection pool or resource saturation. However, search results do not contain current deployment logs, pod metrics, or alert timestamps. Missing data: exact time of unresponsiveness, recent deployment details, current pod health status, connection pool metrics, and whether this affects all nodes or a subset. answer_sections.summary: P1. Bet-service node unresponsiveness directly impacts customer betting operations and revenue. Production service outage with immediate customer-facing impact and SLA breach risk. Investigation findings: Historical evidence shows bet-service P1 incidents have followed deployment changes, specifically a retry-policy deployment that caused connection pool saturation and pod unresponsiveness. The pattern indicates that recent deployments to bet-service are a primary suspect. Node unresponsiveness typically stems from resource exhaustion (connection pools, memory, CPU) or pod health probe failures rather than network isolation. Triage plan: 1. Confirm the exact time nodes became unresponsive and cross-reference with any bet-service production deployments in the last 60 minutes. 2. Check bet-service pod health status, readiness probe responses, and restart counts. 3. Inspect connection pool saturation, memory pressure, and CPU utilization on affected nodes. 4. Review recent deployment changes, feature flags, and retry policy or concurrency settings. 5. Validate downstream dependency health (databases, caches, API Gateway) for cascading failures. 6. If deployment correlation is strong and rollback is available, prepare rollback as primary remediation. Escalation recommendation: Escalate immediately to the bet-service owner and incident commander. If a deployment occurred within the last hour, also notify the platform release owner. This is a P1 production outage requiring urgent coordination for rollback decision and execution. Resolution plan: 1. If deployment is within 60 minutes of unresponsiveness start and rollback is available, execute rollback immediately while continuing diagnostics. 2. Freeze further bet-service deployments until incident is resolved. 3. Monitor pod rollout status and readiness probes during recovery. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics (bet placement latency, error rate) return to baseline for at least 10 minutes. 5. Capture logs from affected pods before restart if unresponsiveness persists after rollback. 6. Open follow-up ticket for root cause analysis of the failed release. Business impact: Bet-service outage blocks all customer betting transactions, directly impacting revenue and customer trust. SLA breach is imminent. Historical similar incident (INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10) was classified P1 and required urgent rollback. Confidence and uncertainty: Confidence: Medium-High. Historical pattern strongly suggests deployment-triggered connection pool or resource saturation. However, search results do not contain current deployment logs, pod metrics, or alert timestamps. Missing data: exact time of unresponsiveness, recent deployment details, current pod health status, connection pool metrics, and whether this affects all nodes or a subset. Obtain these immediately to confirm rollback decision.

citations.excerpt: --- doc_id: "historical_deployment_incidents" title: "Historical Deployment Incidents" doc_type: "incident" services: "auth-service, bet-service, api-gateway" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "historical, deployment, rollback" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Historical Deployment Incidents ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-06-10 Bet-service P1 in GIB-UKGC followed a retry-policy deployment. Root cause was retry fan-out causing connection pool saturation. Resolution was rollback, restart of saturated pods, and circuit breaker validation. ## INC-DEPLOY-2026-05-12 Auth-service P2 began minutes after a feature flag rollout. Root cause was token validation logic rejecting a subset of valid sessions. Resolution was feature flag disablement and then code rollback. ## Reuse Guidance If the latest deployment is within one hour of the alert and rollback is available, prepare rollback while continuing dependency checks.

--- doc_id: "deployment_rollback" title: "Deployment Rollback Runbook" doc_type: "runbook" services: "auth-service, api-gateway, bet-service, payment-service" environments: "production, GIB-UKGC, NJ-DGE" severity_applicable: "P1,P2" tags: "deployment, rollback, release, mitigation" last_updated: "2026-06-08" author: "PITER AiOps" version: "1.0" --- # Deployment Rollback Runbook Use this runbook when an incident begins soon after a release and the service has a known rollback path. ## Investigation Steps 1. Identify the latest production deployment for the affected service and dependencies. 2. Compare deployment time with alert start time and customer-impact timeline. 3. Confirm rollback availability, prior version, and database migration compatibility. 4. Check whether a feature flag or configuration revert is safer than code rollback. 5. Announce rollback intent in the incident channel with service, version, and expected impact. ## Triage Decision Tree - Roll back when a release occurred within 60 minutes, metrics degraded after release, and rollback is available. - Prefer feature flag rollback when only a new code path is affected. - Do not roll back database migrations blindly; involve the owning team and DBA if schema changes are present. ## Remediation 1. Freeze further deployments for the affected service. 2. Execute the approved rollback command or CI/CD rollback job. 3. Watch rollout status until all pods are healthy. 4. Validate customer-facing success metrics for at least 10 minutes. 5. Open a follow-up ticket for the failed release before unfreezing deployments. ## Verification - Error rate, latency, and saturation return to baseline. - No new alerts fire for the same service. - The previous version is confirmed in deployment inventory. ## Escalation For P1 rollback, notify service owner, incident commander, and platform release owner. For P2 rollback, notify service owner and release owner.
